CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with a history of right-sided lung adenocarcinoma undergoing CT-guided percutaneous needle biopsy, now with acute onset dyspnea and hypoxia following the procedure. Rule out iatrogenic pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Single anteroposterior portable chest radiograph obtained in the supine position immediately post-procedure.

FINDINGS:
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with the visceral pleural line identified approximately 5.8 cm from the lateral chest wall at the mid-lung level. Near-complete collapse of the right lung is present, with the right lung retracted toward the hilum. There is mild rightward shift of the trachea and mediastinal structures, though no definitive tension physiology is identified at this time. A small right-sided pleural effusion, likely hemopneumothorax, is noted at the right costophrenic angle. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, pneumothorax, or pleural effusion. The cardiac silhouette is mildly displaced to the right but not compressed. A biopsy needle track is identified in the right lower lobe corresponding to the prior procedure site. No rib fractures are identified. Bony thorax otherwise intact.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided iatrogenic pneumothorax with near-complete right lung collapse, likely secondary to recent CT-guided percutaneous needle biopsy.
2. Mild mediastinal shift to the right; clinical correlation for evolving tension physiology is strongly recommended.
3. Small right-sided pleural effusion, consistent with hemopneumothorax.
Urgent thoracic surgery and interventional pulmonology consultation recommended. Repeat imaging following intervention is advised.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with a history of recently placed right-sided central venous catheter via subclavian approach, now with acute onset hypoxia and decreased breath sounds on the right. Rule out iatrogenic pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Single anteroposterior portable chest radiograph obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with a well-defined visceral pleural line identified approximately 5.5 cm from the lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-thorax. Marked volume loss of the right lung is present, with near-complete collapse of the right middle and lower lobes. The right upper lobe remains partially aerated. Mild leftward shift of the mediastinum and trachea is identified, raising concern for early tension physiology. A right subclavian central venous catheter is in place, with the tip projecting over the distal superior vena cava — acceptable positioning. No pneumomediastinum. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or atelectasis. The cardiac silhouette appears mildly displaced to the left but is otherwise normal in contour. No acute osseous abnormalities identified.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided iatrogenic pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the right middle and lower lobes, most likely related to recent right subclavian central venous catheter placement.
2. Mild leftward mediastinal and tracheal shift, raising concern for early tension physiology. Urgent clinical correlation is recommended.
3. Right subclavian central venous catheter tip in acceptable position within the distal superior vena cava.
Emergent thoracic surgery or interventional consultation is strongly recommended for consideration of chest tube placement.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with a history of recent right-sided thoracentesis for malignant pleural effusion, now presenting with acute onset chest pain and hypoxia. Rule out iatrogenic pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Single anteroposterior portable chest radiograph obtained in the semi-upright position.

FINDINGS:
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with a well-defined visceral pleural line identified approximately 5.5 cm from the lateral chest wall at the mid-lung level. Near-complete collapse of the right lung is present, with the collapsed lung parenchyma retracted toward the right hilum. There is mild leftward shift of the trachea and mediastinum, raising concern for early tension physiology. A small residual right pleural effusion is noted at the right base, consistent with the known malignant effusion. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation or pneumothorax. The cardiac silhouette is mildly displaced to the left but not significantly enlarged in contour. No pneumomediastinum identified. Bony thorax is intact without rib fractures. Subcutaneous emphysema is noted along the right lateral chest wall at the thoracentesis site.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with a history of lung adenocarcinoma on chemotherapy, presenting with acute right-sided chest pain and hypoxia following CT-guided percutaneous lung biopsy performed 2 hours prior.

TECHNIQUE: Single anteroposterior portable chest radiograph obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line measuring approximately 5.5 cm from the lateral chest wall at the mid-lung level. Near-complete collapse of the right lung is identified. Mild rightward tracheal deviation is noted; however, there is no frank contralateral mediastinal shift to suggest tension physiology at this time. A small associated right apical component is present. A chest tube is not yet in place. The left lung demonstrates no focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. There is mild streaky left basilar atelectasis. The cardiac silhouette is mildly obscured by patient positioning but appears normal in size. Subtle cortical irregularity along the right sixth rib posterolaterally may represent a biopsy-related artifact versus minor fracture; clinical correlation recommended. Visualized osseous structures are otherwise intact.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided iatrogenic pneumothorax with near-complete right lung collapse, complicating recent CT-guided percutaneous biopsy.
2. Mild ipsilateral tracheal deviation without definitive mediastinal shift; tension physiology cannot be excluded clinically and requires close monitoring.
3. Left basilar atelectasis, minor.
4. Cortical irregularity of the right sixth rib posterolaterally — correlate with biopsy site; repeat dedicated rib series may be obtained if clinically indicated.
Urgent thoracic surgery consultation and chest tube placement are recommended.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 19-year-old tall, thin male with no significant past medical history presenting with acute onset right-sided chest pain and mild shortness of breath following a coughing episode. Rule out pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: PA and lateral chest radiographs obtained in the upright position.

FINDINGS:
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with a well-defined visceral pleural line identified approximately 5.8 cm from the lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-thorax. Marked collapse of the right lung is present, estimated at approximately 55–60% volume loss, with the right lung retracted toward the hilum. No significant mediastinal shift is identified; the trachea remains midline. The left lung is well expanded and clear without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or atelectasis. No pleural effusion is seen on the right. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. The hemidiaphragms are symmetric in position. No rib fractures or osseous lesions are identified. The soft tissues are unremarkable without evidence of subcutaneous emphysema.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided primary spontaneous pneumothorax with approximately 55–60% collapse of the right lung, in a clinical context consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a young male.
2. No mediastinal shift or evidence of tension physiology.
3. No associated pleural effusion or underlying pulmonary parenchymal abnormality identified.
Urgent thoracic surgery or interventional pulmonology consultation recommended for chest tube or aspiration management.

The left lung is clear without focal consolidation or pleural abnormality. Previously described right lower lobe mass is partially visualized, though assessment is limited due to adjacent collapse.
